column2: Does pulseq automatically apply gradient pre-emphasis or does the user need to define pre-emphasis?

column2: Is EGG gating implemented for cardiac MRI sequences? How does it work? Is the triggering adaptive in case of heartrate variability throughout the scan?

As for the first option 'gating-type' navigators, we actually don't have a plan right now.

column4: there are some limits, but they are very dificult for us to assess. In general, there is this libBalance library from Siemens, which should be able to give you more advanced predictions, but in my experience it becomes unstable for longer sequences and maybe we don't engage all possible/relevant checks. But, from what I've heard, it also has a thermal model...

column2: how is specifying the type of an RF pulse used later on e.g. ‘excitation’ or ‘refocusing’?
column4: it is used for k-space calculation, e.g. 'excitation' resets the k-space position and 'refocusing' reflects it about the origin of k-space. Also on the scanner (Siemens-specific), you can scale RF pulses based on their type, e.g. scale down refocusing pulses without changing excitation or preparation pulses.

column2: If you wanted to substitute trapezoids for waveforms in the gradient blocks, could you using PulSeq?

column2: I have a question about cross-vendor sequence translation using Pulseq. If a pusleq sequence is developed on Siemens and then implemented on GE or Philips, should we expect the timing and waveforms to be exactly identical? Since vendors have different hardware constraints (gradient/RF raster times, slew limits, system dead times), exact timing may not be preserved. If we want to keep sequences as consistent as possible across vendors, is designing on a common base timing grid, like a shared multiple of raster times, or is there a better strategy to ensure cross-vendor consistency?
column4: Yes, designing using a comon block/event duration raster is a good idea to ensure identical timing across vendors. Same for the dead/ringdown times -- you can design for the worst case if perfect  harmonization is the goal. Differences due to waveform raster times are inevitable (e.g., 4us on GE vs 10us on Siemens) and should be kept in mind.

column2: I have a question about cross-vendor sequence translation using Pulseq. If a pusleq sequence is developed on Siemens and then implemented on GE or Philips, should we expect the timing and waveforms to be exactly identical? Since vendors have different hardware constraints (gradient/RF raster times, slew limits, system dead times), exact timing may not be preserved. If we want to keep sequences as consistent as possible across vendors, is designing on a common base timing grid, like a shared multiple of raster times, or is there a better strategy to ensure cross-vendor consistency?
column4: In general. multiplatform / multivendor harmonization is a process. With using comon rasters and "worst case" constraints you can establish something I would call "a common baseline". By runing this sequence you can discover quality/signal differences inherent to respective systems. From there on you can itertively optimze your sequences for specific platforms, but now in a more predictable step-wise process. Importantly, you can still share the source code script which generate seq files accross platforms.

column2: Maybe i am wrong, just to confirm, it appears that there is a function to translate trajectory back to gradient strength? Does it take max slew and max grad into account?
column4: there is a function called mr.traj2grad(), which gives you back gradients and optionally slew rate vectors... But, it assumes a constant time stepping for the provided k-space trajectory, so you have to do optimisation / resampling yourself. A very "raw" example how tht could be done you can find in a demo spiral sequence (demoSeq/writeSpiral). Uncortunately, it is pretty much like a "playground" and not a well established tutorial.

column2: does seq.calcRfPower require some sort of reference voltage to be consistent with what is played on the scanner or do we need to scale what is returned by the function?
column4: no, it gives you a response in Hz for B1_rms or Hz^2 for "relative SAR". You can convert the former to uT by multiplying by gamma and then get more or less identical results as the values in the Siemens UI. You need a coil model and the reference voltage to get to real SAR thoug, but we don't have functions for that. I Hear PyPulseq integrates with some kind of advanced SAR library that can even be used for pTX

column2: What is the output of the interpreter? Is it an app on the Host so that you can load  and run .seq directly, or it converts .seq to .dll and .so, for example on the Siemens scanners?
column4: In Siemens systems the pulseq interpreter is compiled once into *.dll and *.so, and installed in the scanner. These are different files from *.seq files which describe the sequence you want to run.

column2: What is the output of the interpreter? Is it an app on the Host so that you can load  and run .seq directly, or it converts .seq to .dll and .so, for example on the Siemens scanners?

column2: In Pulseq, the RF pulse definition corresponds to the complex baseband envelope, not the fully carrier-modulated RF waveform. column4: there is both amplitude and phase modulation vectors stored in the Pulseq file, so I'm not sure which information are you missing currently
